title: What Consciousness Is
body:
Consciousness is the fact that experience is happening at all. When you taste coffee, feel pain, hear a chord change, or notice a memory arrive in your mind, there is a first-person quality to that experience that cannot be reduced to a list of neural firings or behavioural outputs alone. Philosophers call this subjective character the hard problem of consciousness: why should physical processes in the brain give rise to an inner life?

The study of consciousness sits between neuroscience, philosophy of mind, psychology, and increasingly artificial intelligence. Neuroscience looks for neural correlates of consciousness, the brain activity patterns that reliably accompany awareness. Philosophy asks what we even mean by consciousness, whether it can be explained in physical terms, and whether subjective experience can ever be fully captured from the outside. Psychology examines attention, perception, memory, and self-awareness as they relate to conscious life.

Several theories attempt to explain consciousness, but none has won consensus. Integrated Information Theory argues that consciousness depends on how much information a system integrates. Global Workspace Theory treats consciousness as a broadcast mechanism, where information becomes conscious when it is made available to many specialised processes. Higher-order theories say a mental state becomes conscious when the brain represents that state to itself. Each theory captures part of the picture, but the core mystery remains unresolved.

That unresolved mystery is precisely why consciousness matters. It shapes how we think about personhood, moral status, suffering, selfhood, and even the possibility that future machines could be conscious. Consciousness is not a side topic. It is one of the central questions in science and philosophy because it sits at the boundary between objective description and lived reality.
keyPoints: Consciousness refers to subjective experience — the felt, first-person quality of being aware
The hard problem asks why physical processes in the brain produce experience at all
Neuroscience, philosophy of mind, psychology, and AI all contribute to the study of consciousness
Integrated Information Theory, Global Workspace Theory, and higher-order theories are major competing explanations
Consciousness is different from intelligence, attention, and behaviour, even though they often overlap
The question of machine consciousness is now a practical ethical issue, not just science fiction
